story:
Joe rises before dawn, laces his boots, and kisses his sleeping wife, Nancy. He checks on their little girl, Melissa—bright pink walls, steady breath, all is well—and slips out the front door to go to work. But as the door closes, he’s standing in a dim, bleach-scented hallway, carpet dizzying, only one cold white-blue light burning. His hands are suddenly wrinkled. The door behind him isn’t his home; it’s a clean white door marked 7. Panic rattles him. A nurse appears—Kimberly, RN, Charge Nurse—soft voice, tired patience. “You’re home, Joseph,” she says, guiding him into a room he almost recognizes. In the mirror he’s an old man. “You are 85 years old. You have Alzheimer’s. This is Restful Oaks.” He denies it, accuses her, sinks into a recliner molded to his shape, and waits for a chance to bolt.

When Kim steps away, Joe slips out and follows the handrail, searching for an exit. Door 6, door 5, a blank door—freedom? He backs through it and—there’s Nancy, radiant in a blue dress, tugging his arm. He looks down: tuxedo, matching tie, younger hands. “It’s Mel,” Nancy says. “She’s locked herself in. She’ll only talk to you.” Joe gently coaxes his daughter from behind a heavy wooden door, finds her in a wedding dress with mascara streaks, trembling about the life she’s about to choose. He sits with her, breathes with her, and tells her how he knew Nancy was the one: You just know it in your heart. He promises to love her no matter what she decides. She smiles, braver. He opens the door to bring her to Nancy—and everything vanishes. He’s back in the long hallway. The exit door won’t open. He hammers the keypad until his hand bleeds, crying, “Let me out!”

Kim finds him there, wrings pity out of frustration, and bandages him up. In the sunny common room, a heavy-set man in a wheelchair rolls in, barking, “Room, attention!” under a Korean War cap. He calls himself Sarge. They bond over the locked door. Sarge whispers a plan: during smoke breaks, a nurse escorts him outside; if Joe comes instead, they might slip away. Tomorrow, they agree. They will go home.

That night, another door becomes a portal. Joe steps from his restroom into a bright hospital corridor where happiness hums. Nancy presses flowers into his hands. In a quiet labor room, Melissa cradles a newborn: “Grandpa, meet Baby Joe.” The sight breaks him open. He holds his namesake and remembers holding Melissa not so long ago. He steps into the bathroom for a tissue and returns to darkness—Room 7 again. He collapses, sobbing.

In the morning, Joe asks to join Sarge for a smoke. Kim’s face softens. “Joe… Sarge passed away six months ago.” He weeps into her hug. She shares good news: Nancy is coming for date night. Later, Joe waits in the entry with a daisy. The doorway blooms into memory: the little Italian place, Nancy in a black dress with white polka dots, his too-big pinstriped suit, the ring he could barely afford. “Will you marry me?” “Yes, forever and ever, yes.” Back in the present, Kim helps him stand. Joe kisses Nancy’s wrinkled hand, sees the same ring, the same woman, and tells her, with all the love his wandering mind still holds, “Thank you for making me the happiest man who has ever lived. I love you, forever and ever.”
